subtopic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
title: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
summary: Z = 11 i.e., number of protons = 11 A = 23 Number of neutrons = A - Z = 12 Number of electron = 0 (No electron in nucleus) Therefore, the number of protons, neutrons and electrons are 11, 12, 0.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Isotones means number of neutrons remains same.
- Z = Number of protons A = Total number of protons and neutrons ∴ Number of neutrons, N = A - Z
- Z = 11 i.e., number of protons = 11 A = 23 Number of neutrons = A - Z = 12 Number of electron = 0 (No electron in nucleus) Therefore, the number of protons, neutrons and electrons are 11, 12, 0.

title: Mass-Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Given, $$ A_{1}=240 $$ $$ \mathrm{A}_{2}=\mathrm{A}_{3}=120 $$ $$ \mathrm{BE}_{1}=7.6\mathrm{M e V} $$ $$ \mathrm{BE}_{2}=\mathrm{BE}_{3}=8.5\ \mathrm{MeV} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathrm{BE}&=\mathrm{BE}_{2}+\mathrm{BE}_{3}-\mathrm{BE}_{1}\\&=8.5+8.5-7.6\\&=17-7.6\\&=9.4\mathrm{~MeV}\end{aligned} $$
- Given: $$ m=0.5\ g=0.5\times10^{-3}\ kg $$ Einstein's mass-energy equivalence relation. $$ E=m c^{2} $$ where, $c = \text{velocity of light} = 3 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/s}$ $$ \begin{aligned}E&=0.5\times10^{-3}\times(3\times10^{8})^{2}\\ &=4.5\times10^{13}J.\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} 1 \\ + \quad 6 \\ \hline 16 \end{array} $$
- The value of energy, $$ Q=2(4\times7.06)-7\times(5.60) $$ $$ Q=(8\times7.06-7\times5.60) $$ $$ Q=56.48-39.2 $$ $$ Q=17.28MeV $$ $$ Q=17.3MeV $$
- First increases and then decreases with increase in mass number.
- According to the question. $$ m=1\ \mathrm{u},c=3\times10^{8}\ \mathrm{m s}^{-1} $$ if, $$ E=931~\mathrm{M e V},1\mathrm{u}=931~\mathrm{M e V/c^{2}} $$ ∴ Binding energy, B.E. $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\text{Binding energy per nucleon,}\\=\frac{39.10}{7}=5.58\simeq5.6MeV\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=0.042\times931\\&=39.10MeV\end{aligned} $$
- Mass defect, $$ \Delta\mathbf{m}=\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{p}}+(\mathbf{A}-\mathbf{Z})\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{Z}) $$ Binding Energy, $$ \mathbf{BE}=\Delta mc^{2} $$ ∴ $$ \mathbf{B}\mathbf{E}=[\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{p}}+(\mathbf{A}-\mathbf{Z})\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{n}}-\mathbf{M}\left(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{Z}\right)]\mathbf{c}^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{Z})=\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{p}}+(\mathbf{A}-\mathbf{Z})\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}}-\frac{\mathbf{B}\mathbf{E}}{c^{2}} $$
- B.E. = [ZM_{p} + (A - Z)M_{n} - M(A, Z)]c^{2}
- Energy released. $$ \begin{aligned}&Q=(B.E.)_{product}-(B.E.)_{reactant}\\&Q=(28-4.4)MeV\\&\quad=23.6MeV\\ \end{aligned} $$
- Energy in given reaction = B.E. of products - B.E. of reactants = c - (a + b) = c - a - b
- Binding energy, BE = $ [NM_n + ZM_P - M(N, Z)c^2 $ $$ \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{N},\mathrm{Z})=\mathrm{NM}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{Z}\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}-\frac{\mathrm{B}}{c^{2}} $$
- According to Einstein, mass-energy equation, $$ \mathrm{E}=m c^{2}. $$ Taking mass, $$ m=1\mathrm{a m u} $$ $$ =1.66\times10^{-27}kg $$ and $$ c=3.0\times10^{8}~\mathrm{m/s} $$ Therefore, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=(1.66\times10^{-27})\times(3\times10^{8})^{2}\mathrm{J}\\&=1.49\times10^{-10}\mathrm{J}\\&=\frac{1.49\times10^{-10}}{1.6\times10^{-13}}\mathrm{~MeV}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}(\because1MeV&=1.6\times10^{-13}J)\\&=931.25MeV\end{aligned} $$ Hence, 1 amu ≈ 931 MeV.

title: Nuclear Energy
summary: A moderator's function is to slow down the fast-moving secondary neutrons created during fission, because only slow-moving neutrons can initiate the fission reaction.
body: **Key points from solved questions** - Slow neutrons are more effective in causing fission in $ {}^{235}_{92} $U because they have a higher probability of being captured by the uranium nucleus, leading to fission. An $ \alpha $-particle is the nucleus of a helium atom. Beta particles are high electrons or positrons that are ejected from nucleus by some radionuclides during beta-decay. X-rays have longer wavelength than gamma rays. - Water has high specific heat capacity so that it can absorb a significant amount of heat energy without undergoing a large increase in temperature. In nuclear reactors, efficient heat transfer is needed to manage the high amounts of thermal energy produced during nuclear fission. - The reaction of Uranium is: $$ {\bf\Phi}_{92}^{235}\mathbf{U}+{\bf\Phi}_{0}^{1}n\to{\bf\Phi}_{36}^{89}\mathbf{K}\mathbf{r}+{\bf\Phi}3{\bf\Phi}_{0}^{1}n+\mathbf{X}_{Z}^{\mathrm{A}} $$ According to law of conservation. (Atomic number) $ _{Reaction} $ = (Atomic number) $ _{Product} $ $$ 92+0=36+7 $$ ↓ $$ Z=92-36=56 $$ Similarly, (Atomic mass) Reactant = (Atomic mass) Product $$ 235+1=89+A+3\times1 $$ $$ A=144 $$ The element is $ {}^{144}_{56}Ba $. - Energy released per $ u = \left(\frac{0.02866}{4}\right) $ (931 MeV) = 6.675 MeV - From the question, Here, $ \mathrm{power}, P = 1000\ \mathrm{W} $ $ \mathrm{Energy} $ per hour = $ 1000 \times 3600\ \mathrm{J} $ $ \mathrm{Energy} $ per fission = 200 MeV $ = 200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}\ \mathrm{J} $ No. of fission per hour, $ n = \frac{1000 \times 3600}{200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}} $ \therefore Mass per hour = $ \frac{n}{N} \times 235 $ $$ \begin{array}{l}\begin{array}{r l r}&{=\frac{1000\times3600\times235}{200\times1.6\times10^{-13}\times6.02\times10^{23}}} \\ &{=43.9\times10^{-6}~\mathrm{g}=40~\mu\mathrm{g}}\end{array}\end{array} $$ - Thermal K.E. $ \geq $ Electrostatic P.E. - Mass of $_{1}H^{2}=2.014478$ amu Mass of $_{2}He^{4}=4.00388$ amu Mass of deuterium $2\times2.01478=4.02956$ amu Energy equivalent $_{2}^{2}H=4.02956\times1.1=4.4$ MeV Energy equivalent to $_{2}^{4}He=4.00388\times7=28$ MeV Energy released = 28 - 4.4 = 23.6 MeV - The mass of fission products is always less than mass of parent nucleus. - For nuclei having A > 56, binding energy of nucleus gradually decreases. - Product is more stable so that its mass is less than the sum masses of reactants i.e., $ m_{3} < (m_{1} + m_{2}) $. - Solar energy $ \rightarrow $ fusion of protons into helium. - In nuclear fusion, lighter nuclei merge to form a heavier nuclear. - On the basis of a liquid drop model of the nucleus, nuclear fission can be explained. - A moderator's function is to slow down the fast-moving secondary neutrons created during fission, because only slow-moving neutrons can initiate the fission reaction. The moderator should be made of a light substance that does not absorb neutrons. Heavy water, graphite, deuterium, paraffin and other materials can act as moderators.

title: Objective I
summary: **$\beta^-$ decay**: a neutron converts to a proton inside the nucleus: $$n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$$ The nucleus emits an **electron** (beta particle) and an **antineutrino**.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The **mass number** $A = Z + N$, where $Z$ is the number of protons (atomic number) and $N$ is the number of neutrons. It gives the total number of **nucleons** (protons + neutrons) in the nucleus. It is not exactly the mass in amu (due to binding energy and mass defect), but it is close. Answer: (C).
- The binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) curve peaks at **iron-56** ($\approx 8.8\,\rm MeV/nucleon$). This peak means: - Lighter nuclei ($A < 56$): **fusion** releases energy (moving toward the peak). - Heavier nuclei ($A > 56$): **fission** releases energy (splitting moves toward the peak). Iron is the most stable nucleus — no net energy is released by fusing or fissioning iron. Answer: (C).
- The number of undecayed nuclei follows **exponential decay**: $$N(t) = N_0\,e^{-\lambda t}$$ Activity $A = \lambda N$, so: $$A(t) = A_0\,e^{-\lambda t}$$ where $\lambda$ is the decay constant. The **half-life** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2/\lambda$: after each half-life, the activity halves. Answer: (B).
- From $N = N_0\,e^{-\lambda t}$, setting $N = N_0/2$: $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} \implies \lambda t_{1/2} = \ln 2 \implies t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$ The **mean life** $\tau = 1/\lambda = t_{1/2}/\ln 2 \approx 1.44\,t_{1/2}$. Answer: (B).
- An $\alpha$-particle is $^4_2$He (2 protons, 2 neutrons). In $\alpha$-decay: $$^A_Z X \to {}^{A-4}_{Z-2} Y + {}^4_2\rm He$$ Mass number decreases by **4**, atomic number decreases by **2**. Example: $^{238}_{92}$U → $^{234}_{90}$Th + $^4_2$He. Answer: (C).
- **$\beta^-$ decay**: a neutron converts to a proton inside the nucleus: $$n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$$ The nucleus emits an **electron** (beta particle) and an **antineutrino**. Mass number $A$ stays the same; atomic number $Z$ increases by 1. Example: $^{14}_6$C → $^{14}_7$N + $e^-$ + $\bar{\nu}_e$ (radiocarbon dating). In $\beta^+$ decay: proton → neutron + positron + neutrino ($Z$ decreases by 1). Answer: (B).
- **Nuclear fission** (e.g., $^{235}$U + $n$): a heavy nucleus absorbs a neutron and becomes highly unstable, splitting into **two medium-mass daughter nuclei** (fission fragments) plus typically 2–3 free neutrons and a large release of energy ($\sim 200\,\rm MeV$ per event). The released neutrons can trigger further fissions — a **chain reaction** — which is exploited in reactors and nuclear weapons. Answer: (B).
- On the BE/A curve, light nuclei (like H, He) lie much lower than iron. When they fuse, the product has much higher BE/A — the gain per nucleon is large. For heavy nuclei fissioning, the gain per nucleon (moving from $A\approx238$ toward $A\approx56$) is smaller. Thus, **per unit mass**, fusion releases more energy than fission. Answer: (B).

title: Objective II
summary: - **A** ✓: For any single nucleus, decay is random — we cannot predict exactly when it will decay.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: Total baryon number (mass number) is conserved in all nuclear reactions. - **B** ✓: Total charge (atomic number = proton number) is conserved. - **C** ✗: Mass is **not** conserved separately — the mass defect corresponds to the energy released ($E = \Delta mc^2$). **Mass-energy** is conserved, not mass alone. - **D** ✓: Total energy (including rest-mass energy $mc^2$) is conserved. Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- - **A** ✓: For any single nucleus, decay is random — we cannot predict exactly when it will decay. Only probabilities can be assigned ($\lambda\,dt$ = probability of decay in $dt$). - **B** ✓: For a large ensemble, the law of large numbers gives a precise exponential decay $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. - **C** ✗: The decay constant $\lambda$ is determined by nuclear physics (nuclear force, energy levels). Chemical bonding affects electron orbitals, not the nucleus — $\lambda$ is essentially unchanged by chemical environment (with very rare exceptions in electron-capture decay). - **D** ✓: In $\gamma$-decay, the nucleus transitions from an excited state to a lower state, emitting a photon. No change in $Z$, $N$, or $A$. Answer: (A), (B), (D).

title: Size of The Nucleus
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Nuclear radius, $ \mathrm{R} = R_0 A^{\frac{1}{3}} $ $ \mathrm{R} \propto (\mathrm{A})^{\frac{1}{3}} $ $ \frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} = \left(\frac{125}{64}\right)^{1/3} $, $ \frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} = \frac{5}{4} $
- Radius of nucleus is, $ \mathrm{R} = \mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{A}^{1/3} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \mathrm{R} \propto \mathrm{A}^{1/3} $ $$ \begin{aligned}&\therefore\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Al}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Te}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{Al}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{Te}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\left(\frac{27}{125}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{3}{5}\\ &\quad\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Te}}=\frac{5}{3}\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Al}}\\ &\because\quad\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Al}}=3.6\mathrm{~fm}\\ &\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Te}}=\frac{5}{3}\times3.6=6\mathrm{~fm}\\ \end{aligned} $$
- We know that, (C) we know that, For Al, Nuclear Radius where, A = mass number $\Rightarrow$ $R = R_{0}A^{1/3}$ $=R_{0}(27)^{1/3}=3R_{0}$ since, nuclear radius is 3.6 fm (given) $\Rightarrow$ $R_{0}=\frac{3.6}{3}=1.2$ fm For Cu, $R=R_{0}A^{1/3}=1.2\times(64)^{1/3}$ $=4.8$ fm.
- Density of nuclear matter is independent of mass number, so the required ratio is 1:1.
- Nuclear radii. $$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_{0}\mathrm{A}^{1/3} $$ where. $$ R_{0}=1.2\ f m $$ or $$ \mathrm{R}\propto(\mathrm{A})^{1/3} $$ $$ \frac{R_{Be}}{R_{Ge}}=\frac{A_{Be}^{\frac{1}{3}}}{A_{Ge}^{\frac{1}{3}}} $$ Given $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G e}}=2\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B e}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{Be}}^{\frac{1}{3}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{Ge}}^{\frac{1}{3}}} $$ $$ \mathrm{A_{G e}}^{1/3}=2\times(9)^{1/3} $$ or $$ \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{Ge}}=2^{3}\times9=8\times9=72 $$ ∴ Number of nucleus in Ge is 72. $$ \begin{array}{l}9.\ (D)\ \frac{\text{Volume of atom}}{\text{Volume of nucleus}}=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi(10^{-10})^{3}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi(10^{-15})^{3}}\\ =10^{15}\end{array} $$

title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- $\Delta m=238.050-234.043-4.003=0.004$ u. $Q=0.004\times931.5\ \text{MeV}=3.726\ \text{MeV}\approx3726\ \text{keV}.$
